**Heading 270° (facing the building directly — the critical shot):** I can clearly see a **two-story brick building** with distinctive red/green painted trim on the entrance surround. There is a **full-perimeter wrought-iron security fence** with a gated pedestrian entry. A tall wooden privacy fence runs along the side yard (visible in heading 90°). The building has what appears to be a ground-floor entrance with a recessed doorway.

**What I notice:**
- The building is definitively a **two-flat residential structure** — two stories, two apparent residential windows on each floor, standard Chicago two-flat footprint
- The colored paint on the entry surround (red/green) *could* be an attempt to mark it as a business/school entrance, but there is **no visible signage**, no provider name, no license number posted

**Visual Assessment — 1800 S Lawndale:** This looks like a **residential two-flat being operated as a Day Care Center**. The fence may be a childcare compliance adaptation, but the building shows no commercial/institutional indicators. Combined with the GIS data (property class 212, 2,024 sq ft, built 1896), this is a **130-year-old residential two-flat with a DCC license for 32 children**. The capacity math technically clears (max ~37), but the setting raises serious questions about fire code compliance, egress capacity, ADA access, and outdoor play space requirements. **Confidence: MODERATE-HIGH concern.**

**Satellite assessment — 2446 S Pulaski:** The physical footprint appears large enough to support 102 children. - 17 × 35 sq ft = 595 usable sq ft minimum

The GIS says 3,243 sq ft total building on a 3,755 sq ft lot. The building appears to nearly fill the entire lot footprint. The maximum available is ~512 sq ft.** This is a **physical impossibility for outdoor space compliance**, regardless of indoor square footage.

The max indoor capacity (60 children from my earlier calculation) is technically fine for 17. But the outdoor space constraint appears **impossible to satisfy on this lot.**

Furthermore, this is a **two-story residential building on a residential block** operating as a DCC. While the capacity is modest (17), the outdoor play space appears physically impossible.

## 🔑 1400 S KOLIN AVE — VILLAGE OF HOPE LEARNING CENTER — SATELLITE ANALYSIS

The building has an irregular roofline with what looks like a peaked/hip section in the center and flat sections extending to either side. This is **not a residential building**. The footprint is substantial.

**Pixel measurement:**
- The main building appears to span approximately **195 px wide × 165 px deep**
- 195 × 0.211 = **41.1 meters wide = ~135 feet**
- 165 × 0.211 = **34.8 meters deep = ~114 feet**

**This changes things substantially.** The building at 1400 S Kolin is **not an apartment unit**. It is a **large, single-story institutional or commercial building** occupying roughly a quarter of a city block. The "#1408" in the address now reads differently — it may be a suite number within this complex, or it may be a typo/data artifact.

**Additional details visible:**

- **Parking area** visible to the south/west — consistent with a childcare drop-off operation
- The building is surrounded by a **fence perimeter** (visible as a thin boundary line)
- Upper left corner: A smaller building with a darker roof — could be a separate structure on the same parcel or an adjacent property
- The surrounding neighborhood is residential, but this parcel is clearly an institutional/commercial island

**Visual Assessment — 1400 S Kolin (Village of Hope, 70 capacity):** The building is far larger than the street view (2019) suggested. The satellite clearly shows a substantial institutional structure approximately 15,000 sq ft. Max legal capacity at 15,390 sq ft = (15,390 × 0.65) ÷ 35 = **~286 children**. A licensed capacity of 70 is well within the physical limits of this building. The outdoor area appears adequate. The EX tax-exempt classification is consistent with a nonprofit or religious organization. The Google Places listing shows it as operational with positive recent reviews.

**This provider appears PHYSICALLY LEGITIMATE.** The "pending renewal" status and "#1408" address quirk are the only remaining flags — both are administrative, not physical. **Visual concern: LOW. Downgraded from medium concern to LOW.**

## MINI ME BUNCH INC
**Address:** 4323 W Cermak Rd, Chicago, IL 60623
**License Type:** DCC (Day Care Center) | **Status:** License Issued (IL) | **Capacity:** 17
**Flag Type:** Outdoor Space Physical Impossibility — DCC in residential two-flat with lot geometry precluding legally required outdoor play area

#### The Facts
| Data Point | Finding |
|---|---|
| GIS Building Sqft | **3,243 sq ft** |
| GIS Lot Size | **3,755 sq ft** |
| Property Class | **212** — Two-flat residential building |
| Year Built | **1916** |
| Max Indoor Capacity | **60 children** (3,243 × 0.65 ÷ 35) — indoor space *technically adequate* for 17 |
| Required Outdoor Space | **1,275 sq ft** (17 children × 75 sq ft/child, IL DCFS Part 407) |
| Available Outdoor Space | **~512 sq ft maximum** (3,755 sq ft lot − 3,243 sq ft building = 512 sq ft remaining) |
| **Outdoor Space Deficit** | **−763 sq ft (60% below requirement)** |
| SOS Registration | **Not found** — "Inc" in name but no corporate registration located |
| Google Places | 1 review (3 years old), no text, rated 5.0 — minimal online presence |

| Satellite (zoom 19) | Confirms narrow residential lot; rear yard estimated at **~250–300 sq ft** from pixel measurement |

#### The Calculation
```
Required outdoor space (IL DCFS Part 407):
  17 children × 75 sq ft/child = 1,275 sq ft

Maximum physically available outdoor space:
  Lot size (3,755 sq ft) − Building footprint (3,243 sq ft) = 512 sq ft

Satellite pixel estimate of actual rear yard:
  ~22 px wide × ~20 px deep × (0.211 m/px)² × 10.764 sq ft/m² ≈ 210–300 sq ft

Deficit: 1,275 − 300 = 975 sq ft (76% below minimum)
Even using the theoretical maximum available (512 sq ft):
Deficit: 1,275 − 512 = 763 sq ft (60% below minimum)
```

Building codes are physical laws. A 3,755 sq ft lot with a 3,243 sq ft building cannot yield 1,275 sq ft of outdoor play space. This is mathematically impossible regardless of how the remaining lot area is configured.

#### Why This Is Concerning
The outdoor space requirement under Illinois DCFS Title 89, Part 407 is not discretionary — it is a minimum standard for every licensed Day Care Center. A DCC must have 75 sq ft of outdoor play space per child, enclosed and dedicated to childcare use. The parcel geometry at 4323 W Cermak makes this requirement physically impossible to satisfy on-site. A valid DCC license should not have been issued for this location unless:
1. A formal off-site outdoor space agreement was approved by DCFS (unusual and requiring documented approval)
2. The lot size has been mis-recorded in the GIS (possible but would need verification)
3. The building sqft is overstated in the GIS (possible — GIS sometimes includes basement space)

Additionally: a corporate entity calling itself "Inc" with no Secretary of State registration is a secondary flag. Legitimate incorporated Illinois entities should appear in the SOS database.

#### Innocent Explanations
- DCFS may have approved a waiver or an off-site outdoor space agreement (e.g., use of the Cermak Road median parkway, or a nearby park via formal agreement) — this would not be visible in public records
- The building GIS data may include a basement, with the actual above-grade footprint being smaller, leaving more outdoor space available
- The business may operate under a registered name variant not found in our SOS search

#### Recommended Next Steps
1. **Field Inspection:** Measure the actual outdoor play space at 4323 W Cermak. Confirm whether any fenced, dedicated outdoor play area exists
2. Pull the complete DCFS licensing file to determine whether an outdoor space variance/waiver was granted
3. Request Illinois SOS search under all name variants for the operator
4. Verify whether CCAP billing has been submitted at the licensed capacity of 17
5. Review the building's Illinois State Fire Marshal and Chicago Building Department permit history for any licensed commercial conversion work
